filename: 1746-4269-6-20 - peer reviewed research paper on local plants and medicine knowledge.pdf

filename: 1746-4269-6-20 - peer reviewed research paper on local plants and medicine knowledge.pdf
claim: The most frequently used part of Hagenia abyssinica is the flowers, which are used against intestinal parasites such as tapeworms.
evidence_quote: Though all parts of this medicinal plant are important to local communities, the most frequently used and mentioned part are the flowers, which carry anthelmintic properties, and are used against intestinal parasites (e.g. tapeworms).
not_allowed_use: Direct attribution to any candidate woreda
validation_notes: This is a broad conclusion from the paper, not a measured pharmacological efficacy result.

filename: 1746-4269-6-20 - peer reviewed research paper on local plants and medicine knowledge.pdf

evidence_quote: interest towards utilizing the traditional medicine is diminishing among many especially with younger people.

not_allowed_use: Population-level extrapolation beyond the study areas
validation_notes: Qualitative observation from respondents; not a survey estimate of prevalence.

filename: 1746-4269-6-20 - peer reviewed research paper on local plants and medicine knowledge.pdf
claim: The population of Hagenia abyssinica has drastically decreased in Ethiopia due to anthropogenic pressures, including human population growth, land clearing, and selective cutting.
evidence_quote: In Ethiopia the H. abyssinica population has drastically decreased due to the growing pressures from various anthropogenic factors... an increase in human population, leading to settlement changes and land clearing for agriculture, was the main driving factor for the decline in the H. abyssinica population... heavy utilization i.e. selective cutting for (1) timber, (2) furniture, (3) house construction, (4) firewood use (5) medicine and (6) all other uses combined.

citation.locator: Current population of Hagenia abyssinica and Conservation activities

allowed_use: Broad conservation context and qualitative threat framing
not_allowed_use: Standalone ecological trend quantification or site-specific forest condition claims
validation_notes: This is based on informant reports and author interpretation, not independent population monitoring.

evidence_quote: its population is drastically declining, and therefore, they would like to plant seedlings and carry out appropriate management activities. Consequently, they call for support from agricultural bureau or any other development organizations to get seedlings.
not_allowed_use: Evidence of successful intervention outcomes

filename: 1746-4269-6-20 - peer reviewed research paper on local plants and medicine knowledge.pdf

evidence_quote: it is useful to assist communities to document their knowledge. Moreover, averting illegal cutting and allowing natural regeneration of the population by protecting young seedlings from human and livestock destruction can help conserve this species. Creating public awareness and community-based management is timely
allowed_use: Implementation caveat / recommended actions in comparable context
not_allowed_use: Asserting that these measures were implemented or effective

not_allowed_use: Sampling frame for site-specific scoring

source_type: UNFCCC submission / national REDD+ reference level document
claim: Ethiopia’s 2026 FREL/FRL is a national-level reference built on historical average emissions and removals from 2013 to 2017, with a FREL of 27,284,780 tCO2e/yr and an FRL removals reference level of –1,031,504 tCO2e/yr.
evidence_quote: It is defined at the national level and based on the historical average of emissions and removals observed between 2013 and 2017. • Forest Reference Emission Level (deforestation and forest degradation): 27,284,780 t CO₂e per year • Forest Reference Level (removals reference level): –1,031,504 t CO₂e per year
not_allowed_use: Do not use as site-specific evidence or for local scoring/attribution.
validation_notes: Directly stated in summary on page 6.
National-level only; no site attribution.

source_type: UNFCCC submission / national REDD+ reference level document
claim: The submission includes deforestation, forest degradation, and forest gain, and defines forest gain as conversion of non-forest land to forest land at the minimum mapping unit of 0.5 ha, with non-forest land required to have been non-forest for at least five years before activity start.
evidence_quote: This FREL and FRL includes the REDD+ activities deforestation (forest loss), forest degradation and forest gain (enhancement of forest carbon stocks)... Enhancement of forest carbon stocks through forest gain is defined as a direct, human-induced or natural, permanent conversion of non-forest land classes to forest land at a scale of the minimum mapping unit (MMU) of the forest definition of Ethiopia (0.5 ha)... Under the current submission, forest gain is limited to land that has been non-forest for a period of at least five years prior to the start of planting or restoration activities.
allowed_use: National methods context for eligible forest gain accounting in Ethiopia.
not_allowed_use: Do not infer local project eligibility without site-specific land-history evidence.
validation_notes: Clear definitional text on page 10.
Useful for methodology/reference only.

source_type: UNFCCC submission / national REDD+ reference level document
claim: Natural forest restoration in Ethiopia’s FRL/ FREL includes tree planting or natural regeneration without a commercial purpose, and explicitly includes afforestation/reforestation and assisted natural regeneration.
evidence_quote: Natural forest restoration is defined as tree planting or natural regeneration with the intention of restoring natural forest cover, without a commercial purpose. ... It also includes afforestation/reforestation (AR) and assisted natural regeneration (ANR).
allowed_use: National activity-definition context for restoration types in Ethiopia.
not_allowed_use: Do not use to claim any specific site is undergoing these activities.
validation_notes: Explicit definition on page 11.
No local spatial evidence.

source_type: UNFCCC submission / national REDD+ reference level document
claim: The submission excludes enhancement of forest carbon stock in forest land remaining forest land from the current UNFCCC accounting scope, and states that Ethiopia chose this conservative approach because ART-TREES does not currently allow these removals.
evidence_quote: Enhancement of forest carbon stock in forest land remaining forest land (or the opposite of forest degradation) is not accounted for in the current submission... As ART-TREES currently does not allow the inclusion of removals on forest land remaining forest land, Ethiopia decided to also leave this out of the scope of the UNFCCC submission at least for now.
allowed_use: National accounting-scope caveat for Ethiopia’s REDD+ submission.
not_allowed_use: Do not interpret as a statement about on-the-ground absence of forest recovery or removals.
validation_notes: Good implementation caveat; page 11.

source_type: UNFCCC submission / national REDD+ reference level document
claim: Ethiopia’s FRL accounts for AGB, BGB, deadwood, litter, and soil organic carbon, but only CO2 emissions are included; non-CO2 emissions were excluded because fire data were not systematically collected and were considered unreliable for inclusion.
evidence_quote: The Forest Reference Level (FRL) encompasses the following carbon pools: aboveground biomass (AGB), belowground biomass (BGB), deadwood (DW), litter, and soil organic carbon (SOC)... The proposed FREL and FRL consider only CO₂ emissions... because Ethiopia does not systematically collect data on fire occurrence, the available information is not considered sufficiently reliable for inclusion in the FRL.
allowed_use: National methods context for carbon pool and gas coverage.
not_allowed_use: Do not use as evidence of local fire regime or site-specific emissions.
validation_notes: Directly stated on page 12.
Useful for methodological scope only.

source_type: UNFCCC submission / national REDD+ reference level document
claim: Ethiopia derived activity data for deforestation, forest degradation, and forest gain using stratified area estimation from interpreted sample plots, with a stratified random sampling design based on stable forest, stable non-forest, forest gain, and forest loss strata.
evidence_quote: Activity data for deforestation, forest degradation and forest gain from natural forest restoration and commercial forest planting were derived using stratified area estimation based on the interpretation of sample plots... Stratification was informed by a national-level map that distinguished four principal land cover and land cover change categories: stable forest, stable non-forest, forest gain, and forest loss.
not_allowed_use: Do not use as site-scale map evidence or local area estimate.
validation_notes: Clear methods description on page 28.
No local geography.

source_type: UNFCCC submission / national REDD+ reference level document
claim: For restoration accounting, Ethiopia assumes natural forest restoration reaches carbon equilibrium within 20 years and averages total biomass gain over that period; commercial forest planting uses mean annual volume increment from five plantation species plus IPCC conversion factors and root-to-shoot ratios.
evidence_quote: For natural forest restoration, it is conservatively assumed that all carbon pools in tropical systems reach equilibrium within 20 years. Accordingly, the total biomass gain... resulting from the conversion of non-forest land to forestland through assisted natural regeneration is averaged over this 20-year period... For commercial forest planting, a different approach was adopted due to limited biome-specific inventory data. The mean annual volume increment of Ethiopia’s five major plantation species... was applied... converted to aboveground biomass growth using IPCC biomass conversion and expansion factors (BCEFs)... while belowground biomass was estimated using standard root-to-shoot ratios.
not_allowed_use: Do not treat as measured site performance or local growth rates.
validation_notes: Strong methodological caveat; page 43.
Quantitative method, not site-specific outcome.

source_type: UNFCCC submission / national REDD+ reference level document
claim: Removal factors are provided by biome and pre-afforestation land use class; for example, in the Moist Afromontane biome, natural forest restoration removal factors range from 25.76 tCO2e/ha/yr on annual cropland to 37.50 tCO2e/ha/yr on wetlands, while commercial forest planting ranges from 43.39 to 55.13 tCO2e/ha/yr.
evidence_quote: Table 14: Removal factors (Including SOC sequestration) (tCO2e/ha/yr) ... Moist Afromontane ... Annual Cropland 25.76 43.39 ... Grassland 26.74 44.37 ... Wetland 37.50 55.13
not_allowed_use: Do not use as site-specific growth evidence or to infer a candidate site’s biome without spatial corroboration.
validation_notes: Quantitative table on page 44.
Biome-level, not site-level.

claim: The thesis study was conducted in Gimbo District, Kafa Zone, Southern Ethiopia.
evidence_quote: The study had employed in Gimbo district of Kafa Zone, SN.N.P.R.
allowed_use: site-specific contextual understanding for Gimbo District
not_allowed_use: direct attribution to candidate sites outside Gimbo District or geospatial scoring for unrelated sites
validation_notes: Document location is explicit.
No candidate site match provided for this geography.

claim: The abstract reports that farm households mainly used inorganic fertilizer, while also practicing crop rotation, intercropping, grass strip, contour farming, and residue management.
evidence_quote: The majority of farm household respondents use soil fertility management measures to boost up their production. Soil fertility management activity was more dependent on the application of inorganic fertilizer... Farm households practiced crop rotation and intercropping, grass strip, contour farming and residue management in their farm land areas.
allowed_use: qualitative local context on common soil fertility practices in Gimbo District
not_allowed_use: precise impact estimation or attribution to another site
validation_notes: Summary statement from abstract; likely self-reported/aggregate.

claim: The study area had 38 rural and one urban kebeles; 26 kebeles were excluded because they did not extensively practice soil conservation and were less susceptible to erosion due to flat terrain.
evidence_quote: In study site, there were 38 rural and one urban kebeles... 26 kebeles including urban kebele which don’t extensively practiced soil conservation and less suitability to soil erosion because of the flat nature of the landscape are excluded from the selection.
allowed_use: context on sampling and local landscape variability in Gimbo District
not_allowed_use: generalizing the exclusion rule beyond the study area
validation_notes: Sampling-related statement; not a measured environmental statistic.

claim: Three kebeles—Keja Araba, Kaya Kella, and Woka Araba—were selected as sample sites, and the selected kebeles had 1,041 household farmers in total.
evidence_quote: the heads of Keja Araba, Kaya Kella and Woka Araba Kebeles... the total number of household farmers from the selected kebeles were 1041.
allowed_use: site-specific sampling context for Gimbo District
not_allowed_use: use as evidence for non-matching candidate sites
validation_notes: Useful for local implementation context only.

claim: Respondents identified limited use of soil fertility management measures, overgrazing, and plowing steep slopes among the main causes of soil erosion, with limited use scoring highest (mean 4.26).
evidence_quote: Causes of soil erosion... Overgrazing... Plowing steep slopes... High rainfall... Limited use of soil fertility management measures... Damaged conservation structures... Continues cultivation... Limited use of soil fertility management measures 122 1 5 4.26
allowed_use: qualitative evidence on perceived erosion drivers in the study area
not_allowed_use: treated as externally validated causal proof or generalized beyond Gimbo District
validation_notes: Self-reported perceptions, not independent measurement.

claim: In the study area, 26.2% of respondents said their fields suffered severe erosion, and 54.1% said erosion had a severe effect on crop yield productivity.
evidence_quote: The majority of the farm respondents (26.2%) stated that their agricultural fields are suffering with severesoil erosion hazard... about (54.1%) of the total respondents responded that soil erosion has severe effect on crop yield productivity and crop productivity also declining over time.
allowed_use: local perception evidence on erosion severity in Gimbo District
not_allowed_use: quantitative impact estimate for other areas or causal attribution
validation_notes: Percentages are self-reported survey responses.

claim: Farmers rated extra labor for farming, livestock feed shortage, and farmland devastation among the main consequences of soil erosion; extra labor had the highest mean score (4.57).
evidence_quote: Low crop production & productivity, loss of soil fertility, farm soildegradation, livestock feed shortage, Increased weed invasion, and demand extra labor for farming activity are the main consequences of soil erosion in the area... Demand extra labor for farming activity 122 1 5 4.57
allowed_use: context on perceived consequences of erosion in the study area
not_allowed_use: use as measured economic loss or apply outside the study area
validation_notes: Based on respondent opinion scales.

claim: Inorganic fertilizer was practiced relatively well, but compost, animal manure, and green manure were practiced poorly according to respondent mean scores.
evidence_quote: the response mean score for inorganic fertilizer... was 3.78... not_allowed_use: direct performance comparison across different sites without QA

claim: The thesis study area is Chiro Woreda in West Hararghe, Oromia Region, Ethiopia, and the study focused on smallholder farmers’ adaptation strategies to climate change.
evidence_quote: This study was conducted in chiro woreda district of west Hararghe Oromia regional state. The study mainly focused on the smallholder farmers’ adaptation strategies towards climate change.

evidence_quote: Farmers had adopted various climate change adaptations; Early and late planting, shifting from cattle to small ruminants, practicing soil and water conservation, having planted drought-resistant crops, practicing decreased livestock, practicing forestation and reforestation, practicing mixed farming, practicing irrigation, and practicing daily labor.

claim: This report is a national Ethiopian biodiversity indicators report developed by a multi-institution taskforce and intended for protected area managers, educational and research institutions, and conservation organizations.
evidence_quote: This book is a national report developed by the Ethiopian National Taskforce drawn from three government institutions (CSA, EWCA and IBC) and a local conservation NGO (EWNHS). The book presents examples of biodiversity indicators developed for selected threatened species and extent of area coverage of protected areas (PAs) in Ethiopia. It is intended to be used by protected area managers, educational and research institutions, conservation based institutions and others.
allowed_use: National biodiversity and conservation context; indicator framework background.
not_allowed_use: Site-level suitability scoring or local biodiversity claims.
validation_notes: National-level framing only; not site-specific.

claim: The indicator set includes six selected species and protected area coverage, and is designed to inform conservation and policy measures.
evidence_quote: The selected indicators represent five threatened mammals and one critically endangered endemic bird and extent of coverage of protected areas (PAs)... The subsequent sections of the book provide detailed interpretations about each of the indicator and what it implies in terms of required conservation and policy measures.
allowed_use: Indicator-framework extraction and national conservation planning context.
not_allowed_use: Inference about any specific candidate site's biodiversity condition.
validation_notes: Lists indicator species, but not site-specific distributions here.

claim: National and regional governments can use the indicators to make biodiversity policy, support decisions, and report policy impacts.
evidence_quote: national and regional governments can use these indicators to help make policies for biodiversity conservation and sustainable use and to seek support and justification for their decisions, and to report on the impact of their policies.
not_allowed_use: Operational or site-level decision support claims.

evidence_quote: Land use information can be used to study food security at national, regional and global levels, to develop solution for natural resource management issue such as salinity, water quality, and deforestation and for planning and policy formulation. Land use directly affects land and triggers process such as land degradation, desertification and loss of biodiversity.
not_allowed_use: Direct attribution to a specific site without local evidence.

not_allowed_use: Estimate of any local protected-area proportion or site boundary condition.

evidence_quote: Nearly half of the total land is (41 percent) is non arable land... and about 43 percent of the total land area is arable... Since the recent past, protected areas coverage is increasing in which 15 percent of the land is covered by national parks, wildlife sanctuaries and reserves, controlled and open hunting areas and community conservation and about one percent of land shares water surface.

evidence_quote: Continuous loss of habitat due to high-altitude subsistence agriculture represents the major threat to the very existence of Ethiopian wolf... Recent population decline of the Ethiopian wolf in Bale is mostly due to disease epizootics, with road kills and shooting as secondary threats.

not_allowed_use: Generalize to other sites without locality-specific evidence.

claim: The authors explicitly caution that the information is spatially and temporally bounded and that interpretive scope will change with new data.
evidence_quote: the information provided herein is spatially and temporally bounded. That is with new and updated information, the interpretive scope of indicator species will also gain value.

not_allowed_use: Use as direct evidence of current conditions.
validation_notes: Important limitation affecting all extracted facts.

claim: The report defines deforestation as a human-induced, permanent conversion of forest land to another land use or land cover within the spatial resolution of the satellite imagery used.
evidence_quote: Deforestation/Forest loss is defined as a human-induced, permanent conversion of forest land to another land use or land cover within a spatial resolution of the satellite imagery used.
allowed_use: National-level methodological reference for how the report operationalizes forest loss.
not_allowed_use: Site-specific attribution of deforestation causes or rates without spatial validation.

Applies at national report level, not to a specific site.

claim: The mapping workflow used imagery acquisition, preprocessing in SEPAL, image classification with Random Forest, post-processing, accuracy assessment, and bias-corrected area estimation using CEO, R-Shiny, and Excel.
evidence_quote: The mapping process includes imagery data acquisition, training data collection, pre-processing (image stacking, clipping, enhancement and mosaicking) through SEPAL, image classification through SEPAL, post-processing, accuracy assessment and generation of bias corrected area estimates using Collect Earth Online (CEO), R-Shiny App and MS Excel ... and Random forest machine learning algorithm were for image classification.
allowed_use: Methodological reference for national forest-cover mapping and MRV workflows.
not_allowed_use: Evidence that a specific local project used this workflow.

evidence_quote: Points where the interpreters had low confidence in the classification were subject to field verification (83 points). Consequently, the total number of reference points used in the accuracy assessment and bias corrected area estimation is 3693 samples.

not_allowed_use: Use as a proxy for validation intensity at any candidate site.
validation_notes: Direct numerical validation detail.

not_allowed_use: Claiming 94% accuracy for a local site or project area.

not_allowed_use: Evidence of implementation at a particular site.

claim: The report says Ethiopia has implemented numerous climate mitigation and co-adaptation projects across sectors, with more projects in water and energy than in forestry and agriculture.
evidence_quote: Numerous projects have been implemented in various sectors for climate change mitigation and co-adaptation. The water and energy sector has the highest number of projects, followed by the forestry and agriculture sector.

evidence_quote: "thirty soil samples (0-20cm depth) collected from five dominant LULC types and two elevation categories... only eight parameters (such as sand content, bulk density, soil pH, organic matter, total nitrogen, available phosphorus, CEC, and carbon to nitrogen ratio) were identified as appropriate soil quality parameters by using principal component analysis (PCA)."
allowed_use: Method reference for soil sampling and indicator selection in watershed studies.
not_allowed_use: Evidence of conditions at candidate sites.
validation_notes: Sampling design is explicit, but the results are site-specific to Bururi.

claim: The dissertation states that forest cover and cultivated lands experienced lower erosion rates, while shrubs, grass, and bare lands experienced higher erosion rates due to overgrazing and vegetation removal.
evidence_quote: "Forest cover and cultivated lands experienced low rate, while shrubs, grass, and bare lands have increased rate of erosion... the higher rate of erosion on shrubs, grass, and bare lands is likely caused by overgrazing and removal of vegetation cover from those land cover types."
allowed_use: Comparative context on erosion susceptibility by land cover type.
not_allowed_use: Assign these erosion relationships to another site without validation.
validation_notes: The causal explanation is explicitly framed for Bururi Catchment.

claim: The study reports that erosion in Bururi Catchment remained above accepted tolerance thresholds, including the 5 to 11 t ha−1 y−1 tolerance range and the national maximum tolerable loss estimate of 18 t ha−1 y−1.
evidence_quote: "This mean value is still greater than the normal soil loss tolerance value (5 to 11 t ha-1 y-1) suggested by Renard et al. (1996) and the national maximum tolerable soil loss estimate (18 t ha-1 y-1) recommended by Hurni (1985)."
allowed_use: Context for judging erosion severity in Ethiopian watershed studies.
not_allowed_use: Use as a direct threshold-based assessment for candidate sites without local measurements.
validation_notes: Threshold comparison is site-specific but the benchmark values are literature-based.

claim: The dissertation recommends prioritizing soil conservation measures in hotspot areas and designing soil and water conservation activities according to erosion severity levels in the catchment.
evidence_quote: "appropriate soil conservation measures should be prioritized in the catchment... Conservation planners have to design proper soil and water conservation activities and implement based on the severity levels of soil erosion in the catchment."
not_allowed_use: As a site-specific recommendation for other locations.
validation_notes: Recommendation is directly tied to Bururi Catchment management planning.
